content.text: Core Principle

attrs.alt: Diagram for When to Use Agents

content.text: Use agents when you want Claude to delegate a task to another isolated process, run it separately, and bring the results back
The clearest one-sentence summary:
"A skill tells the current Claude what to do; an agent is a separate Claude that goes off and does it"
Primary Use Case: Parallelism
Scenario
Why agents work
Running tests while generating documentation
No reason to wait for one before starting the other
Checking multiple data schemas at once
Significant speedup over sequential prompting
Any naturally decomposable work
Multiple sub-agents work independently, results return to main process
Parallelism is what makes agents distinct from commands and skills
Secondary Use Cases
1. Token Optimization
Benefit
How it works
Context encapsulation
Agents retain only necessary output, not entire conversation history
Read-only tasks
Excellent for data collection/consolidation
Large tool outputs
Manage build processes/tests, summarize to highlight only relevant parts
2. Model Selection
"Agents facilitate parallel processing and allow for the selection of specific models tailored for particular tasks"
You can assign different models to different agents based on task requirements.
3. Scope Control
Characteristic
Why it matters
Limited context
Agents only get the prompt provided, not complete conversation
Focused execution
"Less is more" — agents only do as told, no scope creep
Clean context
New background instance handles task with clean slate
When NOT to Use Agents
Don't
Use instead
Simple one-step tasks
Skill is better
Tasks that need full context
Main Claude has larger picture
Creating agents that do everything
Give each agent single, well-defined responsibility
Giving all tools to every agent
Restrict tools to minimum needed
Vague descriptions
Write specific description so Claude knows when to invoke
Decision Framework
Your situation
Use
Want to standardize how you interact with Claude in a session
Skill
Want Claude to delegate to isolated process, run separately, bring results back
Agent
Two independent tasks (tests + docstrings)
Agent (parallelism)
Need extensive codebase exploration
Main context (agents have limited context)
Generating very simple code
Agent
Complex tasks requiring substantial context
Main context with pre-prepared plans
Key Requirement: Specific Descriptions
"Because Claude decides when to auto-invoke based on the description, writing a precise description is one of the most important parts of defining an agent"
Description quality
Result
Vague
Claude either over-invokes it or ignores it
Specific
("run the test suite and report failures — invoke after any code change")
Leaves no ambiguity
Best Practices Summary
Do
Don't
Give each agent a single, well-defined responsibility
Create agents that do everything
Restrict tools to the minimum needed
Give all tools to every agent
Write a specific description so Claude knows when to invoke it
Leave the description vague
Use agents for parallelizable, isolated work
Use agents for simple one-step tasks — use a skill
Commit
.claude/agents/
to git
Keep agents local when the whole team would benefit
Best Practices
Following best practices ensures effective use of When to Use Agents in real-world applications.
Start with the simplest approach that solves your problem before adding complexity.
Test edge cases thoroughly, especially boundary conditions and error states.
Document your implementation decisions for future maintainability.
Monitor performance metrics and optimize based on actual usage patterns.
Follow security best practices and validate all inputs at system boundaries.
Use version control and code reviews for all production changes.
Establish clear error handling strategies before deployment.
Common Mistakes and How to Avoid Them
Understanding typical pitfalls in When to Use Agents helps you avoid costly errors.
Over-engineering solutions when simpler approaches would suffice — start minimal.
Neglecting error handling, leading to silent failures in production.
Skipping testing because the implementation appears straightforward.
Ignoring rate limits and quotas when designing at scale.
Not considering the user experience impact of latency or error states.
Performance and Optimization
Optimizing When to Use Agents for production environments requires attention to key performance factors.
Factor
Impact
Optimization Strategy
Latency
High
Use caching and streaming responses
Throughput
Medium
Implement request batching and connection pooling
Cost
High
Monitor token usage and optimize prompt length
Reliability
Critical
Add retries with exponential backoff
Security Considerations
Security is paramount when implementing When to Use Agents in production systems.
Never expose API keys in client-side code or public repositories.
Validate and sanitize all user inputs before processing.
Implement proper authentication and authorization controls.
Audit logs for all sensitive operations and data access.
Follow the principle of least privilege for all service accounts.
Practical Implementation Guide
A step-by-step approach to implementing When to Use Agents in your projects.
Assess your requirements: Define clear goals, constraints, and success criteria.
Design the architecture: Plan how components interact before writing code.
Implement incrementally: Build a minimal working version first, then iterate.
Test comprehensively: Cover both happy paths and error scenarios.
Deploy carefully: Use staging environments and gradual rollouts.
Monitor and iterate: Collect metrics and improve based on real-world usage.
Testing Strategies
Effective testing ensures your When to Use Agents implementation works correctly under all conditions.
Unit tests: Verify individual functions and components in isolation.
Integration tests: Confirm components work correctly together.
End-to-end tests: Validate complete user workflows from start to finish.
Performance tests: Ensure acceptable response times under load.
Chaos testing: Verify resilience by intentionally introducing failures.
Production Deployment Checklist
Configure proper logging and monitoring before going live.
Set up alerting for error rates, latency spikes, and cost anomalies.
Implement circuit breakers to prevent cascading failures.
Document runbooks for common operational scenarios.
Establish incident response procedures and escalation paths.
Schedule regular reviews of performance metrics and costs.
Advanced Patterns and Techniques
Advanced patterns help you get the most out of When to Use Agents for complex use cases.
Design for observability from the start — add tracing and metrics early.
Use feature flags to gradually roll out changes and minimize risk.
Implement graceful degradation so partial failures don't cause complete outages.
Consider multi-region deployments for high-availability requirements.
Document architecture decisions using Architecture Decision Records (ADRs).
Troubleshooting Guide
When issues arise, follow a systematic troubleshooting approach to identify and resolve them quickly.
Reproduce the issue: Identify the exact steps that trigger the problem.
Check logs: Review error messages and stack traces for clues.
Isolate variables: Change one thing at a time to identify the root cause.
Consult documentation: Verify your implementation matches the expected usage.
Seek community help: Share minimal reproduction cases when asking for help.
Integration Patterns
Integrating When to Use Agents with existing systems requires careful consideration of data flows and system boundaries.
Pattern
Use Case
Considerations
Synchronous
Real-time user interactions
Requires low latency, blocks the caller
Asynchronous
Batch processing, long tasks
Non-blocking, needs status polling
Event-driven
Reactive systems, notifications
Loose coupling, eventual consistency
Overview

When to Use Agents is a fundamental concept for anyone working with Claude and the Anthropic ecosystem. Understanding it deeply allows you to build more effective applications, write better prompts, and get more value from Claude's capabilities. This section provides a thorough exploration of the topic, covering both the theory and practical application aspects that you'll encounter in real-world usage.

Whether you're a beginner just starting out or an experienced developer looking to deepen your knowledge, this guide walks through everything you need to know about When to Use Agents. We'll cover the core concepts, walk through concrete examples, address common questions, and show you how to apply what you've learned in practical scenarios.
Why This Matters

Understanding When to Use Agents is important for several reasons. First, it forms the foundation for many other concepts in the Claude ecosystem. When you grasp this topic well, you'll find that more advanced subjects become significantly easier to understand and work with.

Second, practical knowledge of When to Use Agents directly improves the quality and efficiency of your work. Whether you're building AI-powered applications, crafting prompts for specific use cases, or integrating Claude into existing systems, this knowledge pays dividends in every interaction.
Saves time by avoiding common mistakes and misunderstandings
Enables more effective use of Claude's capabilities for your specific needs
Provides the foundation for understanding more advanced topics
Helps you make better architectural and design decisions
Reduces troubleshooting time when issues arise in production
Improves communication with teammates about AI integration strategies
Core Concepts
To fully understand When to Use Agents, it helps to break it down into its core components. Each component builds on the previous ones, creating a complete picture of how everything fits together.
Fundamental Principles

At its core, When to Use Agents is about how Claude processes and responds to different types of inputs and contexts. The fundamental principles that govern this behavior are consistent across all use cases, even though the specific implementation details may vary.
Consistency: Claude applies the same underlying principles regardless of context or phrasing
Context sensitivity: The same input can produce different optimal outputs depending on the surrounding context
Iterative refinement: Most effective usage involves refining your approach based on results
Transparency: Claude's behavior follows predictable patterns that you can learn and leverage
Key Components

The key components of When to Use Agents work together as an integrated system. Understanding each component individually is important, but understanding how they interact is what enables truly effective usage.
Input processing: How Claude receives and interprets your requests
Context management: How previous interactions influence current responses
Output generation: How Claude formulates and formats its responses
Quality control: Built-in mechanisms that ensure response quality and safety
Getting Started: Step-by-Step Guide
Here's a practical, step-by-step guide to getting started with When to Use Agents. Follow these steps in order to build a solid foundation.
Set up your environment: Ensure you have access to Claude through the appropriate interface for your use case (Claude.ai, API, or Claude Code).
Understand the basics: Read through this documentation thoroughly before attempting your first implementation.
Start simple: Begin with the simplest possible example to verify your understanding is correct.
Iterate and experiment: Modify your approach based on results, keeping notes on what works and what doesn't.
Review best practices: Consult the best practices section below to align your approach with proven patterns.
Test edge cases: Once your basic implementation works, test it with edge cases and unusual inputs.
Scale gradually: Only add complexity when your simpler version is working reliably.
Practical Examples
The best way to understand When to Use Agents is through concrete examples. These examples range from simple to complex, illustrating different aspects of the topic.
Basic Example
The following example demonstrates the most fundamental use case for When to Use Agents. This is a great starting point for understanding the core behavior.

# Example: Getting started with When to Use Agents
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Basic usage example
message = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[
        {
            "role": "user",
            "content": "Explain When to Use Agents in simple terms with a practical example."
        }
    ]
)

print(message.content[0].text)
Advanced Example
This more advanced example shows how to use When to Use Agents in a real-world scenario with proper error handling, configuration, and best practices applied.

# Advanced example: Production-ready When to Use Agents implementation
import anthropic
from typing import Optional
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class ClaudeWhentouseagentsHandler:
    """Handles When to Use Agents with production best practices."""

    def __init__(self, api_key: Optional[str] = None):
        self.client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=api_key)
        self.model = "claude-opus-4-5"

    def process(self, input_text: str, max_tokens: int = 2048) -> str:
        """Process input with error handling and logging."""
        try:
            logger.info(f"Processing request of length {len(input_text)}")
            response = self.client.messages.create(
                model=self.model,
                max_tokens=max_tokens,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": input_text}]
            )
            result = response.content[0].text
            logger.info(f"Successfully generated response of length {len(result)}")
            return result
        except anthropic.RateLimitError:
            logger.warning("Rate limit reached, consider reducing request frequency")
            raise
        except anthropic.APIError as e:
            logger.error(f"API error: {e}")
            raise

# Usage
handler = ClaudeWhentouseagentsHandler()
result = handler.process("Your query here")
print(result)
Comparison: Approaches and Trade-offs
Different approaches to When to Use Agents have different trade-offs. This comparison helps you choose the right approach for your specific use case.
Approach
Best For
Advantages
Disadvantages
Simple / Direct
Prototypes, quick tasks
Easy to implement, minimal setup
Less control, harder to scale
Configured / Structured
Production apps
Consistent behavior, maintainable
More setup required
Automated / Integrated
High-volume workflows
Efficient, scalable
Complex to debug, higher initial cost
Hybrid
Complex real-world systems
Flexible, best of both worlds
Requires careful design
Frequently Asked Questions
These are the most common questions about When to Use Agents, based on developer feedback and support requests.
Q: What is the most important thing to understand about When to Use Agents?

A: The most important thing to understand is that When to Use Agents is a system that rewards careful, intentional usage. The more precisely you communicate your intent, the better results you'll get. Start with clear, specific inputs and refine based on the outputs you receive.
Q: How does this interact with other Claude features?

A: When to Use Agents works together with most other Claude features. It's particularly important to understand how it interacts with system prompts, conversation history, and any tools or integrations you're using. In most cases, these interactions are synergistic rather than conflicting.
Q: What are the performance implications?

A: Performance depends on several factors: the complexity of your inputs, the length of your context window, and the Claude model you're using. Generally, simpler and more focused inputs produce faster and more accurate results. For high-throughput use cases, consider batching and caching strategies.
Q: How do I debug issues?

A: When debugging When to Use Agents issues, start by isolating the problem. Simplify your input to the minimum that reproduces the issue, then gradually add complexity back. Check the API documentation for current behavior specifications and use logging to capture the full request-response cycle.
Best Practices
These best practices reflect lessons learned from production deployments and community experience with When to Use Agents.
Be specific and explicit about what you want — vague inputs produce vague outputs
Test with representative examples from your actual use case before scaling
Implement proper error handling for all edge cases and API errors
Use the appropriate Claude model for your use case and budget constraints
Monitor usage and costs from day one to avoid unexpected bills
Keep your prompts and configurations in version control
Document your implementation decisions for future team members
Build in feedback loops to continuously improve your implementation
Common Mistakes to Avoid
Assuming one approach works for all use cases — always test with your specific requirements
Not handling errors — API calls can fail for many reasons; always handle failures gracefully
Over-complicating prompts — simpler is usually better; add complexity only when needed
Ignoring token limits — always account for input and output token constraints in your design
Not testing edge cases — unusual inputs often reveal important behavior patterns
Skipping monitoring — without metrics, you can't improve what you can't measure
Summary

This guide covered When to Use Agents comprehensively: from core concepts and fundamental principles to practical examples, comparison of approaches, and best practices. The key insight is that effective usage comes from understanding the system deeply and applying that understanding consistently. Start simple, test thoroughly, and iterate based on results.

attrs.alt: When to Use Agents: Decision Criteria

content.text: The Core Decision: When to Delegate to the Model

Choosing to build an agent is choosing to delegate decision-making authority to an LLM. This is not always the right choice. The question is not 'could an agent do this?' — the answer is almost always yes. The question is 'does an agent add enough value over a workflow to justify the added complexity and reduced reliability?'

Anthropic's guidance is explicit: always implement workflows where possible and only resort to agents when they are truly required. This is not a statement about the capability of agents — it is a statement about engineering pragmatism. Users need products that work reliably. A workflow that works 95% of the time is more valuable than an agent that works 80% of the time, even if the agent handles a wider variety of inputs.
Five Criteria That Justify Agents
Open-ended task structure:
The problem requires exploring paths that cannot be enumerated in advance. Example: debugging an unknown bug — you don't know which files are relevant until you start looking.
Judgment is required:
The task involves decisions that depend on context and cannot be reduced to explicit rules. Example: code review that must weigh trade-offs between readability, performance, and correctness.
Exploration is inherent:
Making progress on the task requires gathering information that you don't know you need until you find related information. Example: understanding the architecture of an unfamiliar codebase.
Multi-step without a fixed path:
The task involves multiple steps, but the sequence of steps depends on what is discovered along the way. Example: adding a feature to a codebase you haven't seen before.
Adaptation value exceeds reliability cost:
The flexibility of the agent produces significantly better outcomes on the full distribution of inputs, and the cases where it fails can be handled gracefully or are acceptable.
